coredata.dc:title: Carrageenan/polyvinyl alcohol composite film reinforced with spermidine carbon dots: An active packaging material with dual-mode antibacterial activity
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Fan
Zhu, Shuangshuang
Du, Yuhan
Zhe, Taotao
Ma, Kaixuan
Liu, Mengru
Wang, Li
coredata.dc:description: Exploring biopolymer-based antibacterial packaging materials is promising to tackle the issues caused by petroleum plastic pollution and microbial contamination. Herein, a novel packaging material with two antibacterial modes, continuous and efficient, is constructed by dispersing positively charged spermidine carbon dots (Spd-CDs) in a carrageenan/polyvinyl alcohol (CP) composite biopolymer. The obtained nanocomposite film (CP/CDs film) not only gradually releases the ultra-small Spd-CDs but also rapidly generates reactive oxygen species to inhibit the reproduction of E. coli and S. aureus. Benefiting from the complementary advantages of carrageenan and polyvinyl alcohol, as well as the addition of Spd-CDs, the CP/CDs films exhibit high transparency, good mechanical performance, water vapor barrier ability, low migration, etc. The CP/CDs film as a packaging material is validated to be effective in preventing microbial contamination of pork samples. Our prepared nanocomposite film with sustainability and efficient antibacterial properties is expected as food active packaging.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carrageenan
Antibacterial
Packaging

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Mechanical performances of CP/CDs nanocomposite films
Water contact angle and water vapor permeability
Characterization and performances of the CP/CDs nanocomposite films
Attenuated total reflectance-Fourier transform infrared spectrometry
Water contact angle and water vapor permeability

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Ministry of Science and Technology of the People's Republic of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors gratefully acknowledge the financial supports of Talented Program (A279021724) and the Chinese Universities Scientific Fund (No. 2452021123). We also thank Mr. Guoyun Zhang (State Key Laboratory of Crop Stress Biology for Arid Areas, Northwest A&F University, Yangling, China) for his assistance with SEM analysis.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Characterization of Spd-CDs. (a) TEM image (inset: high-resolution TEM image), (b) size distribution, (c) zeta potential, (d) UV–vis absorbance, (e) FTIR spectrum, (f) fluorescence spectrum of Spd-CDs.
SEM images of the surface and cross-section of the CP film and CP/CDs nanocomposite films at different magnifications.

(a) Comparison of functional groups of carrageenan film, PVA film, and CP composite film. (b) Structural comparison of CP film and CP/CDs nanocomposite films. (c) Macroscopic images under natural light and UV transmission spectra of CP film and CP/CDs nanocomposite films. (d) Macroscopic photos under 365 nm UV light and fluorescence spectra of CP film and CP/CDs nanocomposite films.

(a) Mechanical performances of CP film and CP/CDs films. (b) Comparison of the mechanical properties between our work (CP film and CP/CDs nanocomposite films) and some reported biopolymers [7,28–32]. (c) Hydrophilicity, (d) water vapor permeabilities, and (e) thermostability of the CP film and CP/CDs nanocomposite films.

(a-d) UV–vis absorption spectra of the lipophilic food simulant (95 % ethanol aqueous solution) after contacted with CP film and CP/CDs nanocomposite films for different times. (e-f) Migration of CP/CDs3 nanocomposite film immersed in 95 % ethanol aqueous solution for different times.

(a) SEM images of E. coli and S. aureus that treated with Spd-CDs and Spd-CDs plus light irradiation. (b) Photographs of E. coli and S. aureus colonies treated with Spd-CDs with visible light for 15 min and without visible light for 24 h, respectively. (c) Comparison of fluorescence intensity of DCFH-DA (fluorescent indicator for ROS) with different film samples after co-incubation for 10 min under visible light.
Photographs and survival rate (%) of E. coli and S. aureus colonies treated with CP and CP/CDs nanocomposite films with visible light for 15 min and without visible light for 24 h, respectively.
Bacterial contamination of pork samples wrapped in control film (commercially available plastic wrap), CP film, and CP/CDs nanocomposite films for different times.

head.ce:title: Carrageenan/polyvinyl alcohol composite film reinforced with spermidine carbon dots: An active packaging material with dual-mode antibacterial activity

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Food Science and Engineering, Northwest A&F University, Yangling, Shaanxi 712100, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Food Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Food Science and Engineering, Northwest A&F University, Yangling, Shaanxi 712100, P. R. China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry, Guangzhou Key Laboratory of Analytical Chemistry for Biomedicine, South China Normal University, Guangzhou 510006, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Guangzhou Key Laboratory of Analytical Chemistry for Biomedicine

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry, Guangzhou Key Laboratory of Analytical Chemistry for Biomedicine, South China Normal University, Guangzhou 510006, P. R. China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: College of Food Science and Engineering, Northwest A&F University, Yangling, Shaanxi 712100, PR China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Food Science and Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Exploring biopolymer-based antibacterial packaging materials is promising to tackle the issues caused by petroleum plastic pollution and microbial contamination. Herein, a novel packaging material with two antibacterial modes, continuous and efficient, is constructed by dispersing positively charged spermidine carbon dots (Spd-CDs) in a carrageenan/polyvinyl alcohol (CP) composite biopolymer. The obtained nanocomposite film (CP/CDs film) not only gradually releases the ultra-small Spd-CDs but also rapidly generates reactive oxygen species to inhibit the reproduction of E. coli and S. aureus. Benefiting from the complementary advantages of carrageenan and polyvinyl alcohol, as well as the addition of Spd-CDs, the CP/CDs films exhibit high transparency, good mechanical performance, water vapor barrier ability, low migration, etc. The CP/CDs film as a packaging material is validated to be effective in preventing microbial contamination of pork samples. Our prepared nanocomposite film with sustainability and efficient antibacterial properties is expected as food active packaging.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: To reduce the “white pollution” and health hazards associated with traditional petroleum-based plastics, biopolymer films based on polysaccharides, proteins, lipids, and synthetic biodegradable polymers have been extensively investigated because of their biodegradability and biocompatibility [1,2]. Biopolymer films as food packaging materials should protect food from external influences including mechanical damage, chemical and microbial hazards during transportation and storage, so that the food product can maintain its original quality and condition [3]. Among them, carrageenan, a natural marine polysaccharide, is one of the popular biopolymer film materials; carrageenan films have the advantages of biodegradability, smooth surface, and good tensile strength [4,5]. Nevertheless, the low gas barrier capacity and brittleness of carrageenan films restrict their comprehensive research [6]. To overcome these drawbacks, a simple and effective approach is to design composite polymers consisting of two or more components to counteract the weakness of single material. Polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) is a low-cost, non-toxic, biodegradable, and water-soluble polymer with hydroxyl groups distributed on its chain, which provides good miscibility and compatibility. PVA films exhibit high strain and gas barrier performances [7], therefore, blending of PVA with carrageenan as a substrate for packaging materials is expected. In addition, antibacterial property is a key indicator for biopolymer films as food packaging, inhibiting the growth of bacteria could prevent the occurrence of foodborne pathogenic contamination and extend the shelf-life of products.

Carbon dot is a zero-dimensional carbon material with a wide range of raw materials, simple preparation, small size, good water dispersion, and excellent biocompatibility, which are widely used in the fields of sensing, imaging, catalysis, and antimicrobial [8,9]. The superior antibacterial properties of carbon dots are related to (1) their ultra-small scale, which makes carbon dots easier to cross the lipid bilayers and interact with intracellular components of bacteria [10,11]; (2) their abundant surface groups allow them to exhibit different charged properties, which can bind to bacteria membranes via electrostatic interactions, leading to bacterial membrane damage [12,13]; (3) their excellent photodynamic or photothermal effects, in which the carbon dots can produce reactive oxygen species (ROS) or heat under light excitation, thereby rapidly leading to bacterial death [14,15]. Many antibacterial carbon dots synthesized from natural substances (e.g., fresh banana peels and Sophora Japonica extract) as precursors have been used in food packaging investigations [16,17]. The antibacterial activity of these carbon dots is mainly related to their ultra-small particle size and surface groups, while carbon dots that simultaneously generate ROS through a photodynamic strategy are rare in food packaging research. Spermidine, a polyamine widely distributed in living organisms, is positively charged under physiological pH conditions. The as-synthesized carbon dots have excellent antibacterial activity, which may be attributed to their ultra-small particle size and high positive charge capable of disrupting bacterial membranes [18]. In addition, halogen/spermidine-doped carbon dots have been reported to inactivate bacteria by generating ROS under LED light [12]. It is expected that carbon dots prepared with spermidine as a precursor can be used as functional components to realize dual antimicrobial modes (slow-release antimicrobial and ROS-efficient antimicrobial) for food packaging.

Herein, in this work, a sustainable nanocomposite film consisting of carrageenan/PVA (CP) composite material as the film matrix and spermidine carbon dot as the functional component was prepared and its properties as packaging material were investigated. First, spermidine carbon dots (Spd-CDs) with ultra-small size, positive charge, and photodynamic antibacterial activity were synthesized by using spermidine as the precursor, and then homogeneously dispersed into the CP composite biopolymer to prepare smooth and dense films (CP/CDs) by casting method. The introduced Spd-CDs were proven to improve the antibacterial performances of CP film by slow release and efficient ROS generation under visible light, and the antibacterial effectiveness on food product was verified by packaging test on pork samples. Moreover, the optical properties, mechanical performances, water vapor barrier, hydrophilicity, thermostability, and migration of CP film and CP/CDs nanocomposite films were evaluated. This work paves a new avenue for preparing biopolymer-based food packaging material with efficient antibacterial function.

Spermidine trihydrochloride and carrageenan (reagent grade) were obtained from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd., China; polyvinyl alcohol (PVA, alcoholysis degree: 92.0– 94.0 % mol/mol) were offered by Shanghai Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd., China; glycerol was obtained from Kelong Chemical Reagent Factory (Chengdu, China). 2′,7′-Dichlorofluorescin diacetate (DCFH-DA) was obtained from Beijing Solarbio Science & Technology Co., Ltd., China.

Carbon quantum dots from spermidine (Spd-CDs) were prepared by a simple pyrolysis way. Specifically, 0.1 g of spermidine trihydrochloride powder was added to a quartz boat and heated in a tube furnace at 270 °C for 3 h. After cooling to room temperature, the obtained solid residue was dissolved in 10 mL of ultrapure water and sonicated for 1 h. The mixed solution was then centrifuged at 14000 rpm for 15 min to remove large particles, and the supernatant was dialyzed for 24 h (molecular weight cut off = 1000 Da) to remove other impurities. Finally, the purified Spd-CDs solution was stored at 4 °C for further use.

The CP/CDs nanocomposite films were prepared by the following process. Specifically, 3.75 g of PVA, 2 g of carrageenan, and 1 g of glycerol were added to 100 mL of ultrapure water and mixed continuously at 90 °C until homogeneous. Subsequently, different amounts of Spd-CDs (0.16, 0.32, or 0.48 mg) were incorporated and stirred for 30 min to form mixed CP/CDs solutions. These film-forming solutions were degassed and cast on Petri dishes (diameter = 150 mm), which were dried and equilibrated at 25 °C, 50 % RH to obtain nanocomposite films. The resulting films containing different amounts of Spd-CDs were labeled as CP/CDs1, CP/CDs2, and CP/CDs3, respectively; CP films without Spd-CDs were prepared in the same manner as a control.

The morphology of Spd-CDs was characterized by an FEI Tecnai G20 transmission electron microscopy (TEM, USA); the microstructural morphology of the film was measured using scanning electron microscopy (SEM, S-4800, Hitachi, Co., ltd., Japan). UV–vis absorbance/transmittance spectroscopy was obtained using a Shimadzu UV-2500 spectrophotometer (Japan); Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra of the Spd-CDs and films were measured by a Bruker Vetex70 FTIR spectrometer (Germany); size distribution and zeta potential measurements were collected with a laser particle size analyzer (Malvern instruments limited, UK); fluorescence spectra (λex = 365 nm) were recorded using a Horiba FluoroMax-4 spectrofluorometer (America). The thermal degradation process of films was recorded on a thermogravimetric analysis instrument (Shimadzu TA-60WS+DTG-60A, Japan) with a heating rate of 10 °C/min.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanical performances of CP/CDs nanocomposite films
ce:section.ce:para:
The mechanical properties of nanocomposite films were evaluated by a texture analyzer (Stable Micro Systems TA XT Plus, UK). All film samples were processed to a width of 0.5 cm and a length of 8 cm, and each film was subjected to >10 tensile tests. The tensile strength (TS, MPa) and elongation at break (EB, %) of the films were calculated by the following equation, respectively:
TS = F /
x × W
.
EB =
Lf − L 0
× 100 / L 0
Here, F is the stress at break (N), x is the thickness (mm) of film, W is the width (mm) of film, Lf is the elongation length at break, and L0 is the original grasping length of film sample.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Water contact angle and water vapor permeability
ce:section.ce:para:
The water contact angle (WCA) of the films was investigated using an angle measuring instrument (Chengde Jinhe JY-PHb, China). Specifically, 10 μL of water was dripped onto the surface of the film samples, and the water contact angle was recorded and analyzed at different time points. The water vapor permeability (WVP) of the films was determined based on ASTM E96-95 standard method with some modifications. The films were covered on the bottles containing desiccant, and the edges were sealed to ensure that water vapor passed through the film. The bottles were placed in a 90 % RH environment and their weights were recorded every 12 h. The water vapor transmission rate (WVTR, g/m2·s) and the WVP (g·m/m2·Pa·s) of the nanocomposite films were calculated by the following equation:
WVTR = ∆ m /
S × T
WVP =
WVTR × L
/ ∆ P
Here, ∆m is the water vapor migration (g), S is the bottle mouth area (m2), T is the measurement time (s), L is the thickness of films (m), ∆P is the water vapor pressure difference (Pa).

The migration test of nanocomposite films was carried out using 95 % ethanol (v/v) solution as a lipophilic food simulant. The samples (30 mm × 10 mm) were contacted with 3 mL simulant solution for 6 days, and the ultraviolet spectra of the simulant solution were recorded every day. The migration of Spd-CDs from the films was determined using the concentration-absorbance standard curve of Spd-CDs.

The antibacterial performances of the films were evaluated in line with the international standard ISO 22169 with slight modifications, Escherichia coli (E. coli) and Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus) were tested as representatives. First, the film samples were cut into squares with side lengths of 25 mm and 20 mm, respectively, and then sterilized under ultraviolet light. Subsequently, the cultured bacteria were diluted to 105– 106 CFU/mL, the obtained bacterial suspension (100 uL) was placed on 25 mm side length films, and covered with 20 mm side length samples. After co-incubation at 37 °C and 70 % RH for 24 h, the treated bacteria were diluted and coated on LB agar medium at 37 °C, followed by recorded colony count and photos. To evaluate the photodynamic antibacterial properties of the films, the films and bacteria were contacted in the same way, and then exposed to visible light (xenon lamp, PLS-SXE300, Beijing Perfectlight Technology Ltd., China) for 15 min, the treated bacteria was diluted and coated directly. Moreover, the treated bacteria were subjected to SEM after glutaraldehyde fixation, gradual dehydration with ethanol, and critical point drying. The antibacterial efficiency of the films was calculated by the equation:
Antibacterial efficiency
%
=
Nc − N
× 100 / Nc
where NC and N are the colony count of the control group and the tested group, respectively.

The ROS production property of CP/CDs nanocomposite films was evaluated using the fluorescent dye DCFH-DA as the indicator. Stronger fluorescence represents more ROS generation. Specifically, DCFH-DA ethanol solution was first incubated with NaOH solution for 30 min to obtain DCFH solution. Then the film samples (2*2 cm) were added to the DCFH solution and exposed to visible light, the fluorescence spectra (λex = 488 nm) of the probe solution were recorded after 5 min and 10 min, respectively.

Antimicrobial effectiveness evaluation of the films was conducted using fresh pork as samples. Pork samples (3 g) were wrapped with (1) blank, (2) control film (commercially available food cling film), (3) CP film, (4) CP film + light treatment for 20 min, (5) CP/CDs3 film, and (6) CP/CDs3 film with light treatment for 20 min, respectively. All the samples were storage at 4 °C, the packaging films were removed on day 1, day 3, day 5, and day 7, respectively, the microbial contamination, pH, and total volatile basic nitrogen (TVBN) of pork samples were assessed to confirm the antimicrobial efficacy of CP/CDs3 films with or without visible light treatment [19].

All the results were obtained from the mean of three or more parallel groups. The statistical analysis was carried out using analysis of variance and Duncan test (SPSS software, Chicago, USA) with 5 % significance level.

Spd-CDs were prepared by direct pyrolysis of spermidine at 270 °C for 3 h, through dehydration, polymerization, cross-linking, and partial carbonization process [20]. The residue was dispersed in ultrapure water, and purified Spd-CDs were obtained after ultrasonication, centrifugation, and dialysis. As shown in the TEM image (Fig. 1a), the Spd-CDs were well dispersed in water and showed circle shape, with an average size of approximately 4 nm and size distribution between 1– 9 nm (Fig. 1b). The high-resolution TEM images (insets to Fig. 1a) show a d-spacing value of 0.32 nm, corresponding to the diffraction planes of (002) facet [21], indicating that graphitic-like carbon-based material is formed during the carbonization process. Compared to the neutral potential of raw material spermidine (Fig. S1), the zeta potential value of Spd-CDs solution was +40.4 mV (Fig. 1c), this is because the amino groups of spermidine are inherited and condensed on the Spd-CDs during the heating process [12]. Such high positive charge could facilitate the Spd-CDs to interact directly with negatively charged bacterial membranes via electrostatic interactions and disrupt the membrane integrity [18,22], thus improving the antibacterial activity. Due to the small size and high zeta potential, the Spd-CDs possess high colloidal stability so that the Tyndall effect could be seen after 120 days (Fig. S2). The Spd-CDs solution was presented as a clear, homogeneous pale-brown solution under natural light (inset image in Fig. 1d). The ultraviolet−visible (UV–vis) absorption spectrum of Spd-CDs shows a notable absorption peak around the wavelength of 270 nm (Fig. 1d), which is due to the π → π* transitions of conjugated CC bonds, while the shoulder band at 300–450 nm is ascribed to the n → π* transitions of CN bonds [23]. The chemical structure of Spd-CDs was investigated by FTIR spectroscopy. As depicted in Fig. 1e and S3, the spectra of Spd-CDs and spermidine trihydrochloride were consistent, the preparation of Spd-CDs retained the characteristic functional groups of pristine biogenic polyamines, including CH, NH, and CN bonds. Due to quantum confinement, Spd-CDs solution emits bright fluorescence under 365 nm ultraviolet light illumination (inset image in Fig. 1f). The fluorescence emission spectrum of the Spd-CDs solution at excitation wavelength of 365 nm was collected [18]. As depicted in Fig. 1f, the fluorescence intensity of Spd-CDs had a maximum emission wavelength of 440 nm. Thus, the ultrasmall size and positive charged Spd-CDs were successfully prepared.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization and performances of the CP/CDs nanocomposite films

ce:section.ce:para: Macroscopic photographs of CP film and CP/CDs films showed that all film samples were smooth and flat, with no protrusions visible to the naked eye (Fig. S4). From the SEM images of the films at different magnifications (Fig. 2 ), the surfaces of all films were smooth, tight, and dense at the micrometer scale, and there were no visible holes or cracks on the cross-sectional images. These good results may be attributed to (1) both carrageenan and PVA contain a variety of functional groups in their main chains, which are compatible with each other; and (2) the ultra-small particle size and good water dispersibility of Spd-CDs are uniformly incorporated in the carrageenan/PVA matrix without obvious aggregation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Attenuated total reflectance-Fourier transform infrared spectrometry
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the structure of the films and the effect of Spd-CDs doping on the film structure, the film samples were characterized by ATR-FTIR spectroscopy. As can be observed in Fig. 3a, carrageenan film showed characteristic peaks of hydroxyl groups (3273 cm−1), saturated alkyl (2940 and 2841 cm−1), SO groups of sulfate esters (1233 cm−1), glycosidic linkage (1024 cm−1), 3,6-anhydro-d-galactose (918 cm−1) and d-galactose-4-sulfate (843 cm−1) [24]. PVA film showed a broad characteristic peak at 3273 cm−1, related to the stretching vibration of hydroxyl groups, the peaks at 2936 and 2857 cm−1 related to C–H of alkyl groups, two peaks at approximately 1142 and 1090 cm−1 corresponding to C–C and C–O stretching vibrations, and a peak at 1024 cm−1 corresponded C–C–C stretching vibration, respectively [25]. The similarity of the ATR-FTIR spectra of the carrageenan and PVA films supports the good compatibility of the two matrices mentioned above. The characteristic functional groups of carrageenan and PVA films can be observed in the spectroscopy of CP composite film without significant displacement, which indicates that there is no chemical change between the two matrices. From Fig. 3b, the FTIR spectra of nanocomposite films containing different amounts of Spd-CDs were similar to that of CP film, indicating that the incorporation of a small amount of Spd-CDs did not change the functional groups of CP film, and that the compatibility of CP film with Spd-CDs is good [26].

Ultraviolet light can destroy the nutrients in food, so food packaging materials with UV shielding properties can better maintain the quality of packaged food [27]. The UV transmission spectra of CP film and CP/CDs nanocomposite films are shown in Fig. 3c, the UV shielding performances of the nanocomposite films were gradually improved with the increase of the addition of Spd-CDs. The improvement in UV shielding performance is limited due to the simple structure of Spd-CDs, which has no aromatic backbone structure or phenolic structure. It is noted that the addition of Spd-CDs to films resulted in a slight deepening of the color and negligible loss of transparency. In terms of macroscopic photos and the UV transmission spectra, both CP film and CP/CDs nanocomposite films exhibited high transparency, which allows consumers to clearly see the packaged food products. Therefore, this prepared CP/CDs nanocomposite film as a packaging material has the function of display and promotion. The optical performances of CP film and CP/CDs films were further analyzed by fluorescence emission spectra upon the excitation at 365 nm (Fig. 3d). From the macroscopic photographs, the CP film showed almost no fluorescence emission, while the CP/CDs nanocomposite films emitted bright fluorescence. The fluorescence distribution of the nanocomposite films was uniform without dark spots, which again indicates the well dispersion of Spd-CDs in the CP film matrix. The fluorescence spectra showed results consistent with the photographic results, the fluorescence intensity of the nanocomposite films gradually enhanced with the addition of Spd-CDs. All the CP/CDs nanocomposite films showed maximum emission wavelength located at 435 nm without significant displacement. The CP/CDs nanocomposite films combine high transparency and bright fluorescent properties, making them suitable for packaging anti-counterfeiting.

Nanomaterials as functional components can enhance the several properties of composite materials, thus improving their market competitiveness. To verify whether our prepared Spd-CDs own this function, we investigated the effects of incorporating different amounts of Spd-CDs on the mechanical properties, hydrophilicity, and water vapor permeability of the nanocomposite films. It is essential for packaging materials to possess good mechanical properties, which can resist the effects of various static and dynamic factors (such as pressure and impact) and improve durability [33]. Tensile testing was conducted to evaluate the mechanical properties of PVA film, carrageenan film, CP film, and CP/CDs nanocomposite films. As depicted in Fig. S5, the carrageenan films exhibited high tensile strength (∼78 MPa) but were brittle (∼11 % strain), while PVA films showed high strain (>300 %) but lower strength (∼28 MPa). As we designed, due to the respective advantages of these two matrices, the CP composite films displayed well-balanced mechanical capacities, with tensile strength and elongation at break reaching about 40.6 MPa and 40.7 %, respectively (Fig. 4a). Because the simultaneous enhancement of tensile stress and strain is usually contradictory [34], with the addition of Spd-CDs, the mechanical strength of the films enhanced while the elongation at break decreased. The tensile strength of CP/CDs3 nanocomposite film was significantly increased to 51.7 MPa, and the elongation at break was significantly decreased to 30.9 % (p < 0.05). The excellent mechanical performances of the nanocomposite films were also related to the homogeneous dispersion of Spd-CDs in the matrix. Notably, our prepared CP film and CP/CDs films were much stronger and softer than some reported biopolymers (Fig. 4b), including carboxymethyl cellulose, starch, k-carrageenan, gelatin-PVA, konjac glucomannan, chitosan, and pullulan/PVA [7,28–32].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Water contact angle and water vapor permeability
ce:section.ce:para: The hydrophilicity of CP film and CP/CDs films was then assessed. As shown in Figs. 4c and S6, after the water droplets (10 μL) were in contact with the CP film for 5 s, a water contact angle value of about 91.1° was obtained. As the contact time was extended to 50 s and 100 s, the angle decreased to 84.4° and 81.6°, respectively. After the addition of a small number of Spd-CDs, the water contact angles of CP/CDs1 and CP/CDs2 films were not significantly different from those of CP films (p > 0.05). While the water contact angle values of CP/CD3 nanocomposite films were significantly lower than those of CP films (p < 0.05), indicating the hydrophilicity of the films was improved. Compared to conventional petroleum-based plastics, the hydrophobicity of CP films could be improved in the future study, although it would be limited by the hydrophilicity of the polysaccharides and PVA [35]. The hydrophilic packaging materials are suitable to package fruits such as bananas and tomatoes, or dry foods. Then the water vapor permeance (WVP) of the films were tested (Fig. 4d). The CP film showed a WVP of ∼5.47 × 10−10 g·m·m−2·Pa−1·s−1, with the gradual incorporation of Spd-CDs, the WVP of CP/CDs1 and CP/CDs2 films significantly decreased to ∼3.64 × 10−10 and ∼3.58 × 10−10 g·m·m−2·Pa−1·s−1, respectively. The improved water vapor barrier performance may be due to the dispersion of Spd-CDs, which reduced the active area of water vapor passage through the CP/CDs films [36]. Subsequently, the WVP of CP/CDs3 nanocomposite film increased to ∼4.3 × 10−10 g·m·m−2·Pa−1·s−1, which may be attributed to the improved hydrophilicity, where the films absorb part of the water and interfere with the WVP determination. Nevertheless, the water vapor barrier ability of CP/CDs films is superior to many reported biopolymer films, such as gelatin, chitosan, and cornstarch [37–39]. Packaging materials with good water vapor barrier ability can prevent water loss of packaged food such as fresh vegetables and fruits, thus achieving better preservation.

Thermogravimetric (TG) curves were collected to determine the thermal stability of the films. As depicted in Figs. 4e and S7, the TG and derivative thermogravimetry (DTG) curves of all film samples are similar and could be categorized into four distinct stages. In the case of CP film, the first stage (20–138 °C) and the second stage (139–235 °C) were the low-temperature degradation stages, where the mass loss of the films was related to water volatilization and pyrolysis of short-chain oligomers. In the third stage (236–319 °C), the glass transition and the breakage of hard chain segments occurred, the 29 % of mass loss could be attributed to the breakage and/or elimination of hydroxyl groups in carrageenan and PVA side chains [40]. The DTG curves exhibit a sharp endothermic peak in this stage, which is induced by the energy required for the carbon chains breakage. In the fourth stage (320–600 °C), soft segments such as polyether between carrageenan molecules and the backbone of PVA undergo thermal degradation [41], triggering a 30 % mass loss. The pyrolytic behavior of the CP/CDs nanocomposite films did not change significantly compared to that of CP film, probably due to the low incorporation of Spd-CDs.

To investigate the release behavior of Spd-CDs from the nanocomposite films, we calculated their migration using 95 % ethanol as a fatty food simulating liquid. Figs. 5a-d and S8 display the UV–vis absorption spectra of the simulated fluid after contact with CP film and CP/CDs nanocomposite films for different times. The release of Spd-CDs was positively correlated with the contact time and the number of Spd-CDs added. Fig. 5d clearly shows that Spd-CDs were gradually released from the CP/CDs3 film within 24 h. With the contact time from 0 h to 24 h, the migration gradually increased to about 0.11 mg/dm2; and after the contact time was extended to 6 days, the migration reached about 0.15 mg/dm2. The migration of Spd-CDs in 24 h was higher than that of Spd-CDs from day 1 to day 6. Due to the low addition of Spd-CDs, even if all of the Spd-CDs in CP/CDs3 nanocomposite film could migrate into the packaged product, the migration would be only 0.85 mg/dm2, which is less than one-tenth of the international migration limit (10 mg/dm2) [42]. Such low addition and migration are ascribed to the ultra-small size and high activity of Spd-CDs, which helps to improve their application prospects.

Ultrasmall Spd-CDs with highly positive charge (zeta potential = 40.4 mV) feature could exhibit good antibacterial effect by interacting with bacterial membranes (membrane potential ca. −100 to −150 mV) and disrupting their integrity. [43] To verify this, the SEM images of bacteria that treated with Spd-CDs and Spd-CDs plus visible light were collected. As shown in Fig. 6a, Spd-CDs aggregated on the bacterial surface due to the electrostatic interaction, leading to the crumpling of the bacterial surface and the disruption of the cell membrane; under visible-light irradiation, the Spd-CDs had a stronger disruptive effect on the bacterial membrane. And from the results of spread plate tests (Fig. 6b), the Spd-CDs solution showed favorable and sustained bacterial inhibition against E. coli and S. aureus during 24 h; with the assistance of visible light, the Spd-CDs solution showed >99 % antibacterial ability after 15 min of light treatment. The effective antibacterial activity is attributed to that Spd-CDs could generate ROS under visible light, and the generated ROS have no obvious genotoxicity to bacterial cells [12]. To verify the antimicrobial activity of Spd-CDs in the film matrix, we first measured the ROS generation capacity of CP/CDs nanocomposite films under visible light using the fluorescent dye DCFH-DA as the indicator of total ROS production. The stronger fluorescence represents more ROS generated. From Figs. 6c and S9, the ROS generation of the nanocomposite film gradually improved with the increase of Spd-CDs addition and the extension of irradiation time. The ROS generation capacity of Spd-CDs under light irradiation is related to the electron or energy transfer process [12]. And the ROS play a vital role in inhibiting bacteria through oxidative damage to DNA, RNA, proteins, and lipids of cells [44].

Based on this result, the antimicrobial performances of CP/CDs nanocomposite films were further evaluated. As shown in Fig. 7 , neither with or without light treatment, the nanocomposite films showed concentration-dependent antimicrobial activity. The more Spd-CDs incorporated; the better antibacterial effect of nanocomposite films exhibited. In the absence of light treatment, the CP/CDs nanocomposite films released Spd-CDs slowly, thus achieving continuous bacterial inhibition. In the presence of visible light irradiation, the nanocomposite films generated enough ROS in a short period of time to achieve efficient antimicrobial effect. With these two ways, the antibacterial activity of CP/CDs3 film against S. aureus reached >98 %; while only the slow-release way enabled the CP/CDs3 film to inhibit the growth of E. coli by >97 %. The nanocomposite films showed better photodynamic antibacterial effect against S. aureus than E. coli, which could be ascribed to their different cell membrane properties. Gram-negative bacteria have a peptidoglycan layer with Braun's lipoprotein (in the outer membrane) that could provide additional protection [45]. In any case, the nanocomposite films have excellent and tunable antimicrobial capabilities, with both slow-release approach for sustained microbial inhibition and rapid ROS generation for efficient bacteria damage.

To investigate the antimicrobial effectiveness of our prepared CP/CDs nanocomposite films on food products, fresh pork samples were packaged and analyzed for microbial proliferation. The CP/CDs3 film was selected as the nanocomposite film sample due to its strongest antimicrobial capacity. Fig. 8 shows the bacterial growth of pork samples after being packaged with (1) blank, (2) control film (commercially available food wrap), (3) CP film, (4) CP film with light treatment for 20 min, (5) CP/CDs3 film, and (6) CP/CDs3 film with light treatment for 20 min, respectively. After storing at 4 °C for 24 h, the bacterial counts on all pork samples were very low, attributed to the low temperature inhibited bacterial growth. On the third day of storage, very few bacteria survived in the pork samples packaged in CP/CDs film and treated with light, while bacterial proliferation was observed in the pork samples of the other groups. On the fifth day, the number of bacteria on the pork samples wrapped in the CP/CDs film plus light treatment remained minimal, and the rate of bacterial proliferation was lower in the CP/CDs film group than in the other groups. After seven days of storage, all groups of pork samples were heavily contaminated, except for the two groups of pork samples wrapped in CP/CDs film. During the storage period, the pork samples packaged with CP/CDs films followed by 20 min of light treatment performed optimally in these groups, and the samples packaged with CP/CDs films without light treatment performed the second best, indicating that both the slow-release antimicrobial mode and the light-treated efficient antimicrobial mode of our prepared films were reliable.

In addition, the corresponding physical photographs, pH, and total volatile basic nitrogen (TVBN) values of the packaged pork samples were recorded. As shown in Fig. S10, the pork samples from all experimental groups retained their color and shape during storage, but pH and TVBN values changed significantly. On the first and third days, all pork samples remained fresh with pH < 6.2 and TVBN <15 mg/100 g. With the extension of storage time, the proteins in pork samples decomposed and produced alkaline nitrogenous substances, thus the pH and TVBN values of all samples increased to different degrees. On the fifth day, except for two groups of samples packaged in CP/CDs3 film, the pH and TVBN of pork samples from the other groups exceeded 6.2 and 15 mg/100 g, respectively. And the pork samples packaged in commercially available plastic wrap were already spoiled (TVBN >25 mg/100 g). By the seventh day, the pork samples packaged in CP/CDs films were still edible, while other samples were close to spoilage or already spoiled. In terms of preservation effect, our prepared CP/CDs nanocomposite films have more advantages and potential than the commercially available plastic wrap.

We propose an effective strategy to significantly improve the antibacterial performance of biopolymer film by utilizing carbon dots of biopolyamine origin. The prepared Spd-CDs, with ultra-small particle size and high positive charge, exhibited excellent antimicrobial activity including photodynamic efficient antimicrobial property. With Spd-CDs as the functional component and carrageenan/PVA as the hybrid matrix, the prepared CP/CDs nanocomposite films could prevent microbial contamination by both the slow release of Spd-CDs and the efficient generation of ROS under visible light. Packaging test on pork samples also verified the antibacterial superiority of the films. In addition, the CP/CDs films have high transparency and bright fluorescence, which have the potential for anti-counterfeiting applications. Carrageenan and PVA complemented each other in terms of mechanical properties, outperforming some reported biopolymer films. The addition of Spd-CDs further enhanced the tensile strength and water vapor barrier ability, thereby increasing the marketability of CP/CDs films. By utilizing the high activity of Spd-CDs, only a tiny number of Spd-CDs was required to significantly improve the performances of the films, with migration well below one-tenth of the international maximum limit standard. This research presents a novel way for designing active components to enhance the performances of biopolymers.

Fan Li: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Shuangshuang Zhu: Visualization, Validation, Methodology, Investigation. Yuhan Du: Visualization, Validation, Methodology, Investigation. Taotao Zhe: Visualization, Validation, Investigation. Kaixuan Ma: Visualization, Validation, Investigation. Mengru Liu: Visualization, Validation, Investigation. Li Wang: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Resources, Project administration, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully acknowledge the financial supports of Talented Program (A279021724) and the Chinese Universities Scientific Fund (No. 2452021123). We also thank Mr. Guoyun Zhang (State Key Laboratory of Crop Stress Biology for Arid Areas, Northwest A&F University, Yangling, China) for his assistance with SEM analysis.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: The role of the light source in antimicrobial photodynamic therapy
Chem. Soc. Rev.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M. Piksa, C. Lian, I.C. Samuel, K.J. Pawlik, I.D.W. Samuel, K. Matczyszyn, The role of the light source in antimicrobial photodynamic therapy, Chemical Society Reviews 52(5) (2023) 1697-1722, doi:10.1039/d0cs01051k.
